docs.text: Status of a variable's representation in the target health-data vocabulary named by target_concept_vocabulary (e.g. the OHDSI Standardised Vocabulary, the BioData Catalyst model). existing = a concept id is available now; proposed = a submission is in flight; gap = no concept exists and none is proposed.

docs.title: Enum: Value Data Type (DataTypeEnum)
docs.text: The data type of an exposure value.

docs.title: Enum: Day-Boundary Convention (DayBoundaryConventionEnum)
docs.text: Where the 24-hour day window starts. The single most-omitted slot in the environmental-health literature.

docs.title: Enum: Deposit Repository (DepositRepositoryEnum)
docs.text: Repository hosting a FAIR deposit.

docs.title: Enum: Equation Variant (EquationVariantEnum)
docs.text: Equation variant for a derived heat metric. The applicable subset depends on the heat_metric_family.

docs.title: Enum: Exposure Model Type (ExposureModelTypeEnum)
docs.text: The class of model that produced the values.

docs.title: Enum: Extraction Method (ExtractionMethodEnum)
docs.text: How a gridded value was extracted at a target location.

docs.title: Enum: Geocoding Precision (GeocodingPrecisionEnum)
docs.text: Geocoder precision category, mirroring DeGAUSS geocoder.precision.

docs.title: Enum: Health-Layer Target (HealthLayerTargetEnum)
docs.text: The downstream health-data layer a sidecar links into. Open by design; extend as new targets are supported.

docs.title: Enum: Heat Metric Family (HeatMetricFamilyEnum)
docs.text: Family of heat metric.

docs.title: Enum: Heat-Wave Threshold Definition (HeatWaveThresholdDefinitionEnum)
docs.text: How the heat-wave threshold is defined. Companion slot heat_wave_threshold_specifier captures the concrete value.

docs.title: Enum: Homogenisation Status (HomogenisationStatusEnum)
docs.text: For station-based products, the homogenisation status of values across the record period.

docs.title: Enum: Indoor or Outdoor Regime (IndoorOutdoorEnum)
docs.text: Indoor / outdoor regime for derived heat metrics.

docs.title: Enum: Lag Alignment (LagAlignmentEnum)
docs.text: Whether the values have been pre-aligned to a clinical event window in this ETL. Companion slot lag_alignment_specifier captures the concrete lag pattern (e.g. lag_3_days, distributed_lag_0_21). Relocated here from envar_temporal.

docs.title: Enum: Linkage Strategy (LinkageStrategyEnum)
docs.text: The spatial rule used to read an environmental value out of the exposure surface (a grid, a monitoring network, or a set of administrative areas) and assign it to one patient at their location. This is the core geographic-join decision, and it trades spatial precision against robustness to location error: a point read is precise but breaks if the geocode is wrong, whereas an area or buffer read is coarser but more forgiving. Each value below names the companion fields it makes required.

docs.title: Enum: Missing Data Handling (MissingDataHandlingEnum)
docs.text: How the source handles missing values.

docs.title: Enum: Missing-Value Reason (MissingReasonEnum)
docs.text: Controlled reasons that a slot value is missing. Distinguishes a slot the source genuinely does not produce (terminal) from one we could extract but did not (roadmap) from a bug in the chain.

docs.title: Enum: Partial-Day Attribution (PartialDayAttributionEnum)
docs.text: How boundary / transition days of the patient's trajectory (trip start / end, travel days) are attributed when location changes within a day.

docs.title: Enum: PHI Status (PhiStatusEnum)
docs.text: PHI content of the sidecar.

docs.title: Enum: Provenance Chain Terminus (ProvenanceChainTerminusEnum)
docs.text: The kind of root of a provenance chain.

docs.title: Enum: Solar Radiation Basis (SolarRadiationBasisEnum)
docs.text: Basis used for the solar-radiation input to a heat metric.

docs.title: Enum: Source Native Format (SourceNativeFormatEnum)
docs.text: Format the source ships in.

docs.title: Enum: Table Orientation (TableOrientationEnum)
docs.text: How variables map onto columns in the companion data file.

docs.title: Enum: Target Geography Type (TargetGeographyTypeEnum)
docs.text: Geographic unit the exposure is attached to.

docs.title: Enum: Temporal Aggregation Method (TemporalAggregationMethodEnum)
docs.text: How sub-period values were aggregated. CF cell_methods aligned.

docs.title: Enum: Temporal Resolution (TemporalResolutionEnum)
docs.text: Native temporal grain of the source product.

docs.title: Enum: Uncertainty Type (UncertaintyTypeEnum)
docs.text: Kind of per-value uncertainty.

docs.title: linkml-microschemas-envar
docs.text:
LinkML microschemas for environmental exposure variables (EnVar). Initial scope: heat-related exposures (Tmax, WBGT, heat index, heat-wave flags) and the surrounding provenance metadata needed to load them into OMOP external_exposure with full reproducibility.

This umbrella schema imports the fourteen modules that together define the EnVar metadata layer:

  • envar_common shared enums and slots
  • envar_variable variable identity (CF, UCUM, target-vocab concept, ECTO, ENVO)
  • envar_layout data layout (column bindings into the companion data file)
  • envar_spatial spatial reference, extraction
  • envar_temporal temporal reference, day-boundary convention
  • envar_source upstream source dataset
  • envar_model exposure model character
  • envar_uncertainty uncertainty and quality
  • envar_linkage gridded-to-patient linkage
  • envar_toolrun tool run and W3C-PROV provenance chain
  • envar_heat_metric derived heat metrics (WBGT, HI, UTCI, heat-wave)
  • envar_health_layer health-data-layer linkage (OMOP, BDC, …) and FAIR deposit
  • envar_record the top EnvironmentalExposureRecord composite
  • envar_examples concrete record subclasses (Tmax, Tmin, WBGT, EHD)

URI: https://w3id.org/linkml/microschemas/envar

docs.title: Slot: Address Period Alignment (address_period_alignment)
docs.text:
How the patient's location-over-time (the spatial axis of trajectory resolution) was modelled.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Assuming a single static address smears home-location exposure across days the patient was actually elsewhere; how location-over-time was modelled changes which days get which values and can bias exposure estimates.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: People move house and travel, so "where the patient was" changes over time. This records whether the study used one fixed address, a full address history, or accounted for known trips away from home.

docs.title: Slot: Bias Correction Applied (bias_correction_applied)
docs.text:
Whether and how bias correction has been applied. Usually none for Tmax from reference products.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Pooling bias-corrected and raw values silently mixes apples and oranges. Without this flag, an analyst cannot tell whether two datasets differ because of the environment or because one of them was statistically adjusted.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Sometimes model output is nudged after the fact to better match real observations — that nudging is "bias correction". This slot records whether the numbers were adjusted that way and, if so, which technique was used.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason bias_correction_applied is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "the producer never documented any correction" from an unrecorded answer. Without it, a blank bias-correction field cannot be told apart from missing metadata, weakening any audit of how values were adjusted.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If nobody could say whether the numbers were statistically adjusted, this slot records why that answer is missing — for example, the data producer never documented it.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Bias Correction Is Missing (bias_correction_applied_missing_reason)
Slot: Calendar (CF) (calendar)
docs.text:
CF calendar. gregorian is the default; only matters when a source uses a non-standard calendar (some climate model output does).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Some climate-model output uses non-standard calendars (365-day noleap, 360-day); joining such a series to real-world Gregorian clinical dates without converting shifts daily values progressively through the year — a silent, cumulative misalignment.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Most data uses the ordinary calendar, but some climate models simplify — for example pretending every year has exactly 365 days (no leap days) or twelve 30-day months. If you line those dates up against a real calendar without converting, they slowly drift out of sync.

docs.title: Slot: CF Cell Methods (cf_cell_methods)
docs.text:
CF cell_methods string describing how the value summarises sub-period values, e.g. time: maximum for Tmax, time: mean for daily mean.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A daily maximum and a daily mean of the same quantity carry the same standard name and units; only the aggregation statement tells them apart. Keeping the verbatim CF string also enables round-tripping against CF/NetCDF source products and the CF-consistency triple check over standard_name + cf_cell_methods + units_ucum. Conditionally-Core because the slot is CF-specific by design: it is mandatory when standard_name uses the CF: prefix (the context of SPEC.md validation rule 5), while a non-CF product genuinely has no verbatim cell_methods string to preserve — its vocabulary-neutral aggregation semantics live in the required temporal_aggregation_method.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A "daily temperature" value could be the day's highest reading, its average, or its lowest — very different numbers. This short standard phrase (like "time: maximum") records which one it is, in the exact wording that climate-data software already understands.

docs.title: Slot: Chain Compatibility Assertions (chain_compatibility_assertions)
docs.text:
Declarations that two chain steps are compatible (e.g. daymet@1.0.0 expects geocoder@>=3.0.0 output column schema). Optional but enables strict-mode validation.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Version bumps in one chain step can silently break the assumptions of the next (e.g. a changed output column schema); explicit compatibility declarations make such mismatches machine-checkable in strict mode instead of surfacing later as wrong values.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: These are notes saying "step B was built to accept what step A produces" — like declaring that a plug and a socket follow the same standard, so a checker can catch mismatched pairs automatically.

docs.title: Slot: Clinical Date Assignment Convention (clinical_date_assignment_convention)
docs.text:
The clinical-side mirror of day_boundary_convention (envar_temporal): which timezone / day-boundary rule collapsed the clinical timestamp to the date used in the join. A boundary mismatch between this and the exposure-side day_boundary_convention silently misattributes boundary-hour events to the wrong day, which is what makes the Core day_boundary_convention checkable at all. Metadata about the join — never the clinical timestamp itself; carries no PHI.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: If the clinical side collapsed timestamps to dates with a different day-boundary rule than the exposure side, events near midnight are silently joined to the wrong day's exposure; recording both rules is what makes that mismatch detectable at all.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A hospital visit at 11 pm can count as "today" or "tomorrow" depending on which clock and cutoff you use. This records the rule the clinical data used to turn a timestamp into a calendar date.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason clinical_date_assignment_convention is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes a convention that is undocumented at the source from one the pipeline has not yet extracted — which determines whether the day-boundary cross-check can ever be completed.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the clinical date-rule field is empty, this says why.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Date Convention Is Missing (clinical_date_assignment_convention_missing_reason)
Slot: code
docs.text: The code value as a CURIE

docs.title: Slot: code_label
docs.text: Human-readable label for the code

docs.title: Slot: code_system
docs.text: The code system URI

docs.title: Slot: Anchored Cohort Size (cohort_size_anchored)
docs.text:
Number of distinct persons this exposure record was extracted for. Helps downstream estimate the volume of health-layer rows the sidecar describes.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Gives downstream consumers a volume expectation: without it, a health-layer loader cannot sanity-check whether the number of exposure rows it receives matches the number of persons the record was extracted for, so silent truncation or duplication goes unnoticed.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: This is simply a head count: how many different people this exposure record covers. It lets whoever receives the data check that nothing went missing along the way.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason cohort_size_anchored is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "the cohort size is genuinely unknown" from "the pipeline just did not record it". Without the reason, a null count is uninterpretable and downstream users cannot tell whether to chase the number or accept its absence.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the head count is missing, this slot says why — for example, the tool could have counted but did not, or counting simply does not apply. An explained blank is far more useful than a silent one.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)

Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cohort Size Is Missing (cohort_size_anchored_missing_reason)
Slot: comparator
docs.text: Comparison operator (less than, greater than, etc.) or exact if null

docs.title: Slot: Concept Cross-References (concept_mappings)
docs.text:
Cross-references binding this variable to other vocabularies and ontologies, each as a CURIE. One generic list rather than a slot per standard, so adding a vocabulary is a new prefix, not a schema change. Examples: ECTO:0000012 (Environmental Conditions, Treatments and Exposures Ontology — cross-Monarch / cross-CHORDS alignment), ENVO:01000339 (Environment Ontology — material or process exposed to), LOINC:... / SNOMED:... (where the variable has clinical coverage). Note: the primary downstream health-data binding, with its existing / proposed / gap status, stays in the structured target_concept_* slots — this list is for additional, status-free cross-references.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Cross-references to ontologies (ECTO, ENVO) and clinical codes (LOINC, SNOMED) let the variable be found and aligned across projects and communities; without them, cross-study harmonisation has to be redone by hand. Enrichment only — not needed to reproduce the value.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Different scientific communities keep different dictionaries of concepts. This is a list of "also known as" links pointing to the matching entries in those dictionaries, so people searching any of them can still find this measurement.

docs.title: Slot: Concept Status (concept_status)
docs.text:
Status of this variable's coverage in the target health-data vocabulary (existing / proposed / gap). Makes the vocabulary gap explicit rather than silent. Required.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A downstream system must know whether to expect a concept id. The explicit existing/proposed/gap status makes vocabulary gaps visible and countable rather than silently null — which is what drives vocabulary-extension requests for environmental variables.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A simple status flag saying whether the health-data catalogue already has a code for this measurement, has one in the works, or has nothing yet. It turns a silent gap into an explicit, reportable fact.

docs.title: Slot: Container Image Digest (container_image_digest)
docs.text:
SHA256 of the container image actually used (not just the tag). Reproducibility-critical.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Image tags are mutable, so "same tag" does not mean same code; only the digest pins the exact bytes that ran. It is the strongest reproducibility anchor available for a containerised run.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A digest is an exact serial number for the software bundle actually used — stronger than a version label, which can be reused for different contents. If the bundle changes at all, the serial number changes.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Image Digest Is Missing (container_image_digest_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason container_image_digest is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Without a stated reason, a missing digest is indistinguishable from a pipeline bug; with one (e.g. the runner never resolved the tag) the gap is auditable and fixable rather than silent.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: This note explains why the software bundle's exact serial number was not recorded — often the runner only kept the human-friendly version label and never looked up the precise identifier behind it.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Image Digest Is Missing (container_image_digest_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Image Digest Is Missing (container_image_digest_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Image Digest Is Missing (container_image_digest_missing_reason)

Slot: Reason Image Digest Is Missing (container_image_digest_missing_reason)

Slot: Container Image Repository (container_image_repository)
docs.text:
Container image repository, e.g. ghcr.io/degauss-org/daymet. Null for non-containerised tools.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The repository says where the packaged software lives, so anyone can pull the same container and re-run the step. Without it the image digest has nothing to be resolved against, and re-execution requires guessing where the tool came from.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Containers are self-contained software bundles, and the repository is the public shelf address where a bundle is published — effectively the download location of the exact software package used.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Image Repository Is Missing (container_image_repository_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason container_image_repository is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A blank repository is ambiguous — it could mean "the tool is not containerised" or "we forgot to record it". Stating the reason turns a silent gap into checkable information; a blank is a bug, a null-with-reason is information.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the repository field is deliberately empty, this note says why — for example, the tool simply was not run inside a container. An explained blank can be trusted; an unexplained one is a question mark.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Image Repository Is Missing (container_image_repository_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Image Repository Is Missing (container_image_repository_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Image Repository Is Missing (container_image_repository_missing_reason)

Slot: Coordinate Reference System (CRS) (crs)
docs.text:
Coordinate reference system as an EPSG identifier or PROJ string. Mandatory. E.g. EPSG:4326 for Daymet, EPSG:5072 for NARR Lambert Conformal Conic.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Without the CRS the grid cannot be placed on the Earth correctly: the same coordinate pair points to different physical locations under different reference systems, so a mismatched or missing CRS silently shifts every location and attaches exposure values to the wrong places.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A coordinate reference system is the agreed way of turning numbers into places on the Earth — the same pair of coordinates means different spots under different systems. Naming the system (usually as a short EPSG code) tells software exactly which convention the numbers follow.

docs.title: Slot: Cross-Validated R² (cv_r2)
docs.text: Cross-validated R² for the model as a whole.

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The single most comparable headline number for model skill. Without it a consumer cannot weigh this product against an alternative, or decide whether the model is good enough for the health analysis at hand.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A 0-to-1 score of how well the model's predictions matched reality on data it was not trained on — 1 is perfect, 0 is no better than always guessing the average. "Cross-validated" means the test used held-out data, so the score is honest.

docs.title: Slot: Cross-Validated RMSE (cv_rmse)
docs.text: Cross-validated RMSE for the model as a whole.

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: R² alone hides how large the errors actually are. RMSE states the typical error in the value's own units, which is what determines whether model error is negligible or fatal for a given effect size.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Root-mean-square error — the typical size of the model's mistakes, in the same units as the value itself (e.g. °C or µg/m³). Smaller is better.

docs.title: Slot: Data Completeness Percentage (data_completeness_pct)
docs.text:
Percent of (location, date) cells in the extracted window that have a non-missing value. 0-100.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A quantitative handle on how much of the requested exposure window is actually populated. A series that is 60 % complete supports very different inferences from one that is 100 % complete; without this number, sparse coverage is hidden and averages or exposure windows are computed over gaps as if they were full, biasing the health analysis.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Out of all the days and places you asked about, this is the percentage that actually have a value — 100 means nothing is missing, 60 means four in ten slots are blank. It is a quick honesty check on how full the dataset really is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason data_completeness_pct is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "completeness is genuinely uncomputable" from "it could be derived but the pipeline does not yet do so" — a distinction that tells a data steward whether the gap is a limitation of the source or a fixable pipeline shortfall.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If the completeness percentage is missing, this says why — for example the number could be worked out from the data but the software has not been set up to calculate it yet. It flags the blank as a known to-do rather than an impossibility.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Data Completeness Is Missing (data_completeness_pct_missing_reason)
Slot: Data Layout (data_layout)
docs.text:
Data-layout object binding this sidecar to the columns of the companion data file — see envar_layout. Required: without it a consumer cannot locate the values the sidecar describes.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The sidecar describes values that live in a companion CSV/parquet file; without the column bindings a consumer cannot tell which column holds the values, subjects, or dates, so the metadata is unanchored and the data unusable — hence the slot is required.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: The actual numbers live in a separate data table that travels with this record. This block is the map between the two: it names which column holds the values, which holds the person identifier, which holds the date, and so on.

docs.title: Slot: Dataset & Provenance (dataset_and_provenance)
docs.text: Where the values live and come from — the companion file's column layout, the upstream product, and the exact tool runs that produced the values.

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Collects the record's pointers to the physical data (column bindings) and its computational history (upstream product, tool runs) in one place, so reproducibility and file-mechanics questions are answered together rather than scattered among the scientific fields.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: This section answers two practical questions: where exactly are the numbers (which columns of which companion file), and where did they come from (which upstream dataset, and which software runs produced them)?

docs.title: Slot: datetime
docs.text: Absolute timestamp

docs.title: Slot: Day-Boundary Convention (day_boundary_convention)
docs.text:
Where the 24-hour day window starts. Mandatory. Daymet = local_midnight; PRISM = 24h_ending_1200_GMT; NARR / ERA5 sub-daily = utc_midnight. The single most-omitted slot in the literature and a known source of cross-study disagreement.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The single most-omitted slot in the environmental-health literature and a known source of cross-study disagreement: Daymet's local-midnight day and PRISM's 24h-ending-1200-GMT day slice the same thermometer readings differently, so the "daily Tmax" for the same calendar date can differ between products and lagged analyses can shift by a whole day. It is also the exposure-side half of the day-boundary cross-check against the clinical-side clinical_date_assignment_convention.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When does "Tuesday" start and end for this dataset — midnight local time, midnight in London, or noon-to-noon? Different products genuinely disagree, which changes which hot afternoon lands on which day.

docs.title: Slot: Data-Catalog (DCAT) Distribution URL (dcat_distribution_url)
docs.text:
DCAT-compatible distribution URL for catalogue integration.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: This is the machine-actionable download hook for data catalogues: with it, the deposit can be indexed and fetched by DCAT-speaking catalogue software without human mediation. Without it, the record is findable by humans (via the DOI) but invisible to automated catalogue harvesting.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: DCAT is a standard way for data catalogues — searchable indexes of datasets, like a library catalogue — to describe where a file can be downloaded. This slot holds that direct download address so catalogue software can list and fetch the data automatically.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Distribution URL Is Missing (dcat_distribution_url_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason dcat_distribution_url is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Records whether the absence of a catalogue link is deliberate (the record is simply not catalogued) or a gap to be filled, so automated FAIR-ness assessments do not misreport an intentional omission as missing metadata.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If there is no catalogue download link, this slot explains why — usually because the dataset was never registered in a data catalogue at all.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Distribution URL Is Missing (dcat_distribution_url_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Distribution URL Is Missing (dcat_distribution_url_missing_reason)

Slot: Deposit DOI (Digital Object Identifier) (deposit_doi)
docs.text:
DOI assigned by the deposit repository (e.g. Zenodo).

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The DOI is the permanent, citable identifier of the published deposit — the F in FAIR. Without it the record cannot be cited, found in catalogues, or re-retrieved once the original download link rots.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A DOI is a permanent ID for a published dataset, like an ISBN for a book. Whatever happens to the hosting website, the DOI keeps pointing to the dataset, so people can always find and cite it.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Deposit DOI Is Missing (deposit_doi_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason deposit_doi is null (e.g. pre-deposit).

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Separates "this record was never deposited" from "the DOI is pending". Without it, a missing DOI is ambiguous and a consumer cannot tell whether a citable identifier will ever exist for this record.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If the permanent dataset ID is missing, this slot explains why — for instance the record is circulating before publication and the ID has not been issued yet.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Deposit DOI Is Missing (deposit_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Deposit DOI Is Missing (deposit_doi_missing_reason)

Slot: Reason Deposit DOI Is Missing (deposit_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Deposit DOI Is Missing (deposit_doi_missing_reason)

Slot: Deposit License (SPDX Identifier) (deposit_license_spdx)
docs.text:
SPDX identifier of the license under which the derived exposure record (not the source) is published.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: States, in a machine-readable form, what a reuser is legally allowed to do with the published exposure record. Without it, reuse of the deposit is legally uncertain and cautious downstream users must treat the data as all-rights-reserved — the R in FAIR fails.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A license is the legal permission slip attached to the data — for example "you may reuse this if you credit the authors". SPDX is just a standard list of short codes for licenses (like CC-BY-4.0), so software can read the terms without a lawyer.

docs.title: Slot: FAIR Deposit Metadata (deposit_metadata)
docs.text:
FAIR-deposit metadata, present when the sidecar is intended to travel with a published deposit — see envar_health_layer.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: When the sidecar travels with a published deposit, the DOI, repository, and license are what make the object findable, citable, and legally reusable — omitting them strands a public artifact without citation or reuse terms. Optional because most records are never deposited.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If this record and its data are published in a public archive (such as Zenodo), this block holds the publication details: the permanent DOI link, where it lives, and the license saying how others may use it.

docs.title: Slot: Inherited Redistribution Constraints (deposit_redistribution_constraints_inherited)
docs.text:
Constraints from any input source that pass through to the deposit.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A derived product can carry restrictions from its inputs even when its own license is permissive; without recording them, a deposit may violate an upstream license — for example by redistributing grids the source forbids sharing. An explicit empty list positively asserts that nothing passes through.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Datasets built from other datasets can inherit rules from their ingredients, the way a recipe using someone's secret sauce may come with strings attached. This slot lists any such inherited rules so nobody accidentally breaks them when sharing the result.

docs.title: Slot: Deposit Repository (deposit_repository)
docs.text:
Repository hosting the deposit.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Names where the deposited object actually lives, which determines access routes, retention guarantees, and how the DOI resolves. Without it, the deposit's accessibility (the A in FAIR) rests on the DOI alone and cannot be assessed.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A deposit repository is a public archive for research data — a place like Zenodo where a dataset is published with a permanent ID and kept available long-term. This slot records which archive was used.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Deposit Repository Is Missing (deposit_repository_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason deposit_repository is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Makes an absent repository name interpretable: most records are never deposited, and this slot records that this is deliberate rather than an oversight, so completeness checks do not raise false alarms.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If no archive is named, this slot says why — most often because the record was never meant to be published as a standalone dataset in the first place.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Deposit Repository Is Missing (deposit_repository_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Deposit Repository Is Missing (deposit_repository_missing_reason)

Slot: Derived Heat Metric (derived_heat_metric)
docs.text:
Heat-metric methodology, present when the variable is a derived heat metric — see envar_heat_metric. Omitted for non-heat variables.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Heat metrics like WBGT or Heat Index can be computed by several non-equivalent equations from different inputs, and heat-wave flags depend on the threshold definition; the heat-epidemiology literature flags these choices as the main sources of cross-study disagreement, so omitting the block for a heat metric makes the record uncomparable.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Some heat measures are not read from a thermometer but calculated from several ingredients (temperature, humidity, wind, sunshine) using a chosen formula. This block records which formula and ingredients were used — it is only needed when the variable is one of these computed heat metrics.

docs.title: Slot: domain_of_use
docs.text: Domains where this microschema is applicable

docs.title: Slot: duration
docs.text: Duration as alternative to start/end

docs.title: Slot: Equation Inputs (equation_inputs)
docs.text:
Typed per-input references for a multi-input derived metric (Heat Index from T + RH; WBGT from T + RH + wind + radiation). Each entry names the input's role and points, by provenance_id, to the upstream sidecar carrying that input's full context — it is an index into the lineage, not an inline copy of it.

Option-B decomposition (see the EquationInput class): when the inputs originate from different products and diverge in resolution, day-boundary convention, or temporal aggregation, each input is a full upstream sidecar referenced here and listed as a step in provenance_chain, so the divergence stays explicit and checkable.

Conditionally-Core: optional for a single-input metric, mandatory the moment a metric has more than one input.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Multi-input metrics can silently absorb mismatched inputs — e.g. a Heat Index built from a 1 km local-midnight daily-max temperature and a ~31 km UTC daily-mean humidity. Without per-input references that divergence is invisible and no checker can surface it.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Composite heat metrics are cooked from several ingredients — temperature, humidity, wind, sunshine. This is the ingredient list, where each entry points to that ingredient's own full paperwork instead of hiding it inside the final number.

docs.title: Slot: Equation Validity Range (equation_validity_range)
docs.text:
Validity-range conditions for the equation, serialised as a JSON string. For Heat Index: {"min_temperature_F": 80, "min_relative_humidity_pct": 40} — Rothfusz is undefined below 80 °F / 40 % RH.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The Rothfusz Heat Index is undefined below ~80 °F / 40 % RH; applied outside its range an equation produces plausible-looking nonsense. Without the recorded range, no downstream consumer can flag values computed where the formula does not hold.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Every formula only works within a fence of weather conditions it was designed for. This field writes the fence down, so anyone can check whether a value was computed inside or outside it.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Validity Range Is Missing (equation_validity_range_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason equation_validity_range is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "this equation has no published validity range" from "the range exists but was not captured" — the difference decides whether out-of-range screening is impossible or merely deferred.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Validity Range Is Missing (equation_validity_range_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Validity Range Is Missing (equation_validity_range_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Validity Range Is Missing (equation_validity_range_missing_reason)

Slot: Equation Variant (equation_variant)
docs.text:
For derived heat metrics, the equation variant used. Mandatory for WBGT, HI, and UTCI: a Liljegren WBGT and an ACSM WBGT for the same inputs can differ by 2-3 °C.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: WBGT approximations diverge materially from the reference model — well beyond 2-3 °C in hot-humid conditions — so two studies using "WBGT" with different variants are measuring systematically different quantities; pooling them without this field mixes those quantities silently.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Well-known heat metrics have several published formulas that share one name but differ mathematically — like recipes with the same title from different cookbooks. This records which exact recipe was used to cook the number.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason equation_variant is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "no equation variant exists for this metric" from "the variant was used but never captured" — the first is fine, the second means the record is unusable for cross-study comparison and someone should go back to the producer.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Equation Variant Is Missing (equation_variant_missing_reason)
Slot: Exposure Description (exposure_description)
docs.text: What was measured, for whom, where, when, and how — the generic scientific anatomy shared by every exposure record, whatever the variable.

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Groups the per-value scientific predicates (what, where, when, how, how well) into one reading unit, so a reviewer can assess the exposure claim itself before engaging with file mechanics, provenance, or publication concerns.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Think of this as the "science" section of the record: it collects everything that describes the measurement itself — what quantity it is, where and when it applies, and how it was estimated.

docs.title: Slot: Exposure Model (exposure_model)
docs.text:
The exposure-model object describing how values were produced. Bound to ExposureModel (see envar_model). Readable rename of the profile's methodology anatomy slot, narrowed to the model itself: other methodology-adjacent concerns (source dataset, tool run, provenance chain, derived heat metric) are surfaced as separate envar-extension slots.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The same quantity can be estimated by station interpolation, satellite retrieval, or model reanalysis, each with different inputs and biases; without the model description, values from different products get pooled as if equivalent and their systematic differences stay invisible.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Most exposure values are not direct readings from an instrument at someone's house — they are estimates computed from weather stations, satellites, or models. This block says which estimation method produced the numbers and what went into it.

docs.title: Slot: Cross-Validation R² (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2)
docs.text:
Model cross-validation R², where reported by the producer.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The single most useful one-number quality signal for a modeled product. Omitting it leaves downstream users with no quantitative basis for weighing one exposure product against another or for propagating model skill into their error budgets.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Cross-validation R² is a 0-to-1 score of how well the model predicted values it had never seen during training — closer to 1 is better. A score of 0.90 means the model captures most of the real variation; a low score means treat the numbers with caution.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason exposure_model_cross_validation_r2 is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Separates "the producer never published a skill score" from "we forgot to record it". Without this, a missing R² is ambiguous and reviewers cannot tell an undocumented model from sloppy metadata.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If the quality score is blank, this slot says why — for example, some well-known products simply never publish one. It turns an empty field into an honest answer.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Cross-Validation R² Is Missing (exposure_model_cross_validation_r2_missing_reason)
Slot: Ensemble Member Count (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count)
docs.text:
For ensemble products, the number of members. Null with reason not_provided_by_source for single-realisation products.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Mandatory for ensemble products: the member count determines how the ensemble spread can be interpreted as an uncertainty estimate. Without it, per-value spread statistics cannot be reproduced or sanity-checked.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Some products run the same model many times with slightly different settings and combine the results — each run is a "member". Knowing how many members there were (say, 100) tells you how much the spread between runs can be trusted as a measure of uncertainty.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason exposure_model_ensemble_member_count is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Confirms explicitly that a product is single-realisation rather than an ensemble whose member count was dropped. Without it, a null count is ambiguous and the conditionally-core rule for ensemble products cannot be audited.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Many products come from just one model run, so "number of members" genuinely does not apply. This slot says so explicitly, instead of leaving readers to wonder whether information was lost.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Ensemble Member Count Is Missing (exposure_model_ensemble_member_count_missing_reason)
Slot: Exposure Model Inputs (exposure_model_inputs)
docs.text:
Inputs to the model. For GridMET: ["PRISM monthly normals", "NLDAS-2 sub-daily reanalysis"]. For derived heat metrics, the input variable list (held in DerivedHeatMetric.equation_inputs for typed cases).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Needed to trace what the value actually derives from. Without the input list, a shared bias or gap in an upstream dataset cannot be traced through to the exposure values it contaminated.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A model is only as good as what you feed it. This lists the raw ingredients — station readings, satellite images, other datasets — that went into cooking up the final numbers.

docs.title: Slot: Known Model Biases (exposure_model_known_biases)
docs.text:
Free-text flags of known issues, e.g. "NLDAS-2 coastal Tmax bias up to -1.48 °C", "NARR cold bias at extremes", "Daymet warm bias in summer in some western US regions". The field reviewers care about.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The field reviewers care about most; it bites coastal and sparse-station analyses in particular. Omitting it lets a documented systematic error (e.g. a coastal cold bias) silently propagate into health-effect estimates that reviewers will later reject.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Every model has known blind spots — places or conditions where it is reliably a bit wrong, like running warm in summer or cold at the coast. This slot writes those warnings down so the next person does not rediscover them the hard way.

docs.title: Slot: Methods Paper DOI (exposure_model_paper_doi)
docs.text:
DOI of the methods paper describing the model (Thornton et al. 2022 for Daymet; Abatzoglou 2013 for GridMET).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The reproducibility anchor for how the value was made. Without it, anyone auditing or reproducing the analysis must guess which of several versions of a method the values actually came from.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Scientists publish a paper describing exactly how a model works, and a DOI is a permanent web address for that paper. Recording it here means anyone can look up the full recipe behind the numbers, even years later.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason exposure_model_paper_doi is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "no methods paper exists" from "nobody bothered to record it". Without the reason, a blank DOI silently hides whether the model is undocumented or the metadata is just incomplete.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When a field is empty, it helps to say why. This slot records whether the paper genuinely does not exist, was not shared by the producer, or simply was not looked up.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Methods Paper DOI Is Missing (exposure_model_paper_doi_missing_reason)
Slot: Exposure Model Type (exposure_model_type)
docs.text:
The class of model that produced the values. Daymet = spatial_interpolation; NARR / ERA5 = reanalysis; GridMET = statistical_blend; Brokamp PM = single_machine_learning; Di et al. PM = ensemble_machine_learning.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A measured value, an interpolated value, and an ML-predicted value have different error structures and cannot be pooled naively; this is the field that tells them apart. Without it, users cannot judge whether values are measurements or predictions.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Most "exposure" numbers were never measured at your door: a model estimates them from weather stations, satellites, or physics equations. This field says which kind of machinery produced the number, so you know how much to trust it and what could go wrong.

docs.title: Slot: Extraction Buffer Radius (m) (extraction_buffer_m)
docs.text:
Radius of any spatial buffer applied (e.g. greenspace at 500 / 1500 / 2500 m). Null when no buffer is applied.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: For buffer-based strategies the radius is a hyperparameter that changes the assigned exposure — greenspace within 500 m and within 2500 m of a home are different quantities. Omitting it makes buffer-derived values unreproducible and incomparable across analyses.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Some exposures are summarised over a circle drawn around the patient's home rather than at the exact address — for example, how much green space lies within walking distance. This field gives the radius of that circle in metres; a bigger circle averages over more surroundings.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason extraction_buffer_m is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: An empty buffer field is ambiguous: it could mean no buffer was used or that the radius was simply not recorded. Stating the reason makes the absence deliberate and checkable, so validators and reviewers do not flag a legitimate point extraction as incomplete metadata.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the circle-radius field above is left empty, this field says why — most often because no circle was drawn at all and the value was taken straight at the address. It turns a silent blank into an explicit, trustworthy statement.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (extraction_buffer_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Extraction Method (extraction_method)
docs.text:
How a gridded value was extracted at the patient's coordinates. Default for DeGAUSS daymet / narr is inverse_distance_weighted_4_nearest_cells.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: This is the single biggest lever in turning a grid into a per-person value: different extraction methods yield different exposure values at the very same point. If the method is unknown, exposure values are not reproducible and cannot be compared across studies or tool runs.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A patient's home almost never sits exactly at the centre of a data tile, so a rule is needed to pick or blend nearby tile values into one number for that spot — take the closest tile, average the four nearest ones, and so on. This field records which rule was used.

docs.title: Slot: Extraction Window End (extraction_window_end)
docs.text:
Actual end date the run extracted.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The closing bracket of the slice this run actually pulled; without it a reproducer cannot re-request the same window, and a lag analysis cannot verify that the post-event days it needs were actually extracted rather than silently truncated.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Of everything the dataset covers, this is the last date this particular job actually downloaded — the end of the photocopied page range.

docs.title: Slot: Extraction Window Start (extraction_window_start)
docs.text:
Actual start date the run extracted.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Records the dates this run actually pulled, as opposed to what the product offers; without it a reproducer cannot re-request the same slice, and a lag analysis cannot verify that the pre-event days (e.g. the day before the index date) were actually in the extract.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Of everything the dataset covers, this is the first date this particular job actually downloaded — like noting which pages of a big book you photocopied.

docs.title: Slot: Propagated Geocoding Precision (geocoding_precision_propagated)
docs.text:
Quality category propagated from the upstream geocoder (DeGAUSS precision column).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Geocoding precision determines whether "residence" means the actual house or a ZIP-code centroid kilometres away — which changes which grid cell the patient falls in and therefore their assigned exposure. Propagating it lets analysts filter or down-weight coarsely located records.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Geocoding means turning a street address into map coordinates, and it does not always land on the exact house — sometimes only on the street, the ZIP area, or the city. This records how exact the landing was.

docs.title: Slot: Propagated Geocoding Score (geocoding_score_propagated)
docs.text:
Geocoder score (0-1) propagated from the upstream geocoder so the exposure record knows the spatial precision of its anchor.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The score quantifies how confident the geocoder was in its address match; without it downstream analysts cannot apply quality cutoffs, and poorly matched addresses contaminate the exposure assignment invisibly.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When software converts an address into map coordinates it also rates its own confidence, from 0 to 1. This carries that rating along with the exposure record.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Geocoding Score Is Missing (geocoding_score_propagated_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason geocoding_score_propagated is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Records whether the score was never produced or was produced but dropped by the pipeline — the difference between an upstream limitation and a fixable ETL gap.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the geocoder-confidence field is empty, this says why.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Geocoding Score Is Missing (geocoding_score_propagated_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geocoding Score Is Missing (geocoding_score_propagated_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geocoding Score Is Missing (geocoding_score_propagated_missing_reason)

Slot: Health-Data Integration & Publication (health_data_integration)
docs.text: How the record meets patient data and travels onward — the gridded-to-patient join, the downstream health-layer hooks, and the FAIR deposit.

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Groups everything about the record's contact with people and publication — the gridded-to-patient join, the health-data-layer hooks, and the FAIR deposit — because these concerns share reviewers (data governance, health informatics) distinct from the scientists reading the exposure description.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Environmental values only become health data when they are attached to patients and shared onward. This section covers that last mile: how a place-based value was joined to a person, how it plugs into health databases, and how it is published.

docs.title: Slot: Health-Layer Link Field (health_layer_link_field)
docs.text:
Name of the field in the target health-data layer that carries provenance_id. For OMOP this is external_exposure.exposure_source_value (the default); other layers name their own field.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: This is the join key declaration: it names the exact field in the health-data layer that holds this record's provenance_id. Without it, exposure rows cannot be joined back to patients in the health data model, and a health-side row cannot resolve to its exposure provenance.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Think of the exposure record and the patient database as two spreadsheets that need to be matched up. This slot names the column in the patient-side spreadsheet that holds the matching ID, so anyone can connect the two tables without guessing.

docs.title: Slot: Health-Layer Linkage (health_layer_linkage)
docs.text:
Downstream health-data-layer linkage hooks (OMOP, BDC, …) — see envar_health_layer. Optional: its members are Recommended/Optional, and the Core PHI assertion lives at the record root (phi_status).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Naming the downstream health-data layer (OMOP, BDC, …) and the join field makes the exposure record findable from the clinical side; without it the sidecar and the health records it serves drift apart, and the link must be reconstructed by hand.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Health studies keep patient data in large standard databases. This block notes which of those systems the exposure values were wired into and through which field, so someone browsing the health data can find their way back to this record.

docs.title: Slot: Target Health-Data Layer (health_layer_target)
docs.text:
The downstream health-data layer this sidecar links into. Names the target so health_layer_link_field is interpreted against the right model.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Without naming the target layer, health_layer_link_field is ambiguous — a consumer cannot know which data model the link field belongs to, so the exposure record cannot be reliably resolved from the health side. Naming the target in a slot (rather than baking it into slot names) is also what keeps the schema neutral across health data models.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Health records are stored in different standard formats — for example OMOP, a widely used common format for health records, so tools written for one hospital's data work on another's. This slot simply says which of those formats this exposure record is meant to plug into.

docs.title: Slot: Heat Metric Family (heat_metric_family)
docs.text:
The family of heat metric this variable represents.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Tmax, Heat Index, WBGT, and a heat-wave flag are distinct exposures with distinct health associations; without the family, values from different metrics are indistinguishable and get pooled as if they measured the same thing.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: There are many different "heat numbers" — plain air temperature, composite scores that also fold in humidity, wind and sunshine, or a simple yes/no heat-wave flag. This field says which kind of heat number the record is about.

docs.title: Slot: Heat-Wave Minimum Consecutive Days (heat_wave_min_consecutive_days)
docs.text:
Minimum-consecutive-days rule for heat-wave flags (commonly 2 or 3). Changes which days are flagged.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A 2-day rule and a 3-day rule flag different sets of days from the same temperature series, changing which person-days count as heat-wave-exposed and therefore the effect estimates built on them.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A heat wave means it stays hot for several days in a row — but how many days count as "several" is a choice (usually 2 or 3), and the choice changes which days get flagged.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Consecutive Days Is Missing (heat_wave_min_consecutive_days_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason heat_wave_min_consecutive_days is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "not a heat-wave flag, so no consecutive-days rule applies" from "a rule was applied but not recorded" — only the latter undermines reuse of the flag.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Consecutive Days Is Missing (heat_wave_min_consecutive_days_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Consecutive Days Is Missing (heat_wave_min_consecutive_days_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Consecutive Days Is Missing (heat_wave_min_consecutive_days_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Consecutive Days Is Missing (heat_wave_min_consecutive_days_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Consecutive Days Is Missing (heat_wave_min_consecutive_days_missing_reason)

Slot: Heat-Wave Threshold Definition (heat_wave_threshold_definition)
docs.text:
For heat-wave flags, the definition of the threshold (absolute, percentile-local, percentile-climatological, NWS advisory, ETCCDI).

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: At least seven heat-wave definitions are in active use and none are convertible after the fact; two definitions can disagree on most flagged days, which changes both the exposure series and the resulting mortality estimate.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: There is no universal definition of a heat wave. "Above 35 °C" and "hotter than 95 % of days usually are here" are both in use and flag different days — this field records which rulebook was applied.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason heat_wave_threshold_definition is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "this record is not a heat-wave flag, so no threshold definition exists" from "a definition was used but not captured" — the first is benign, the second makes the flag unusable.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Threshold Definition Is Missing (heat_wave_threshold_definition_missing_reason)
Slot: Heat-Wave Threshold Specifier (heat_wave_threshold_specifier)
docs.text:
Free-form specifier paired with heat_wave_threshold_definition to capture the concrete threshold values (e.g. "35_Cel" for an absolute threshold, "95" for the 95th percentile).

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The definition names the rule but not the number: two records both marked "percentile_local" are still incomparable unless the concrete value (95th vs 90th percentile, 35 vs 40 °C) is recorded here.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: The threshold definition is the type of cutoff; this is the actual number plugged into it — like knowing a speed limit exists versus knowing it is 50.

docs.title: Slot: Indoor or Outdoor Regime (indoor_outdoor)
docs.text:
Indoor / outdoor regime. Mandatory for WBGT (the indoor vs outdoor distinction changes the equation and the health interpretation).

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Indoor and outdoor WBGT use different equations and carry different health interpretations; an indoor formula applied to outdoor conditions (or a regime left unstated) makes the value invalid or uninterpretable for the exposure being claimed.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Heat stress works differently inside and outside — indoors there is no direct sun and little wind — so heat metrics come in indoor and outdoor versions, and you need to know which one you are looking at.

docs.title: Slot: Input File SHA-256 Hash (input_file_sha256)
docs.text:
SHA256 of the input file (CSV / parquet).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The input hash lets anyone verify they are re-running on byte-identical inputs; without it, a "reproduction" may quietly use different data, and disagreements can no longer be traced to inputs versus code.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A SHA-256 hash is a fingerprint of a file — if even one byte changes, the fingerprint changes completely. Comparing fingerprints proves two files are exactly the same without inspecting their contents.

docs.title: Slot: Input Provenance ID (input_provenance_id)
docs.text:
The provenance_id of the upstream sidecar that fully describes this input — its source dataset, spatial / temporal reference, and exposure model. Dereferencing it lets a consumer (or the completeness checker) compare inputs and surface any divergence in resolution, day-boundary convention, or temporal aggregation.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: This is the only machine-followable link from an input back to its full spatial, temporal, and model context; without it, divergences between inputs in resolution, day-boundary convention, or aggregation cannot be detected at all.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Think of it as a tracking number: instead of copying all the details of the ingredient into this record, we store a pointer to the record that already holds them, and anyone can follow the pointer.

docs.title: Slot: Input Role (input_role)
docs.text:
The standard-name local token of this input's physical quantity (the same quantity the referenced sidecar carries in standard_name), e.g. air_temperature, relative_humidity, wind_speed, surface_downwelling_shortwave_flux_in_air.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Without a named role, a consumer cannot tell which physical quantity each referenced input supplied to the equation, so inputs cannot be matched to equation terms and cross-input consistency checks cannot run.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: This labels what each ingredient actually is — temperature versus humidity versus wind — using a shared standard vocabulary so that machines and people read the label the same way.

docs.title: Slot: Input Row Count (input_row_count)
docs.text:
Number of rows in the input.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The row count is a coarse but instant integrity check — a truncated or partially delivered input shows up immediately as the wrong count. It also anchors the input side of input/output cardinality checks.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Just the number of lines of data that went in. If you expected 3 addresses and the count says 2, something was lost before the tool ever ran.

docs.title: Slot: Input Source Short Code (input_source_short_code)
docs.text:
Human-readable short code of the input's source product (e.g. daymet_v4, era5), duplicated from the referenced sidecar for convenience when scanning the record. Not authoritative — input_provenance_id is.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Lets a human scanning the record see at a glance which product each input came from without dereferencing every provenance id; because it is a non-authoritative duplicate, omitting it costs readability, not machine-checkability.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A friendly nickname for the dataset an ingredient came from (like "daymet_v4"), written next to the tracking number so people do not have to look everything up just to get oriented.

docs.title: Slot: interval_end
docs.text: End of the interval

docs.title: Slot: interval_start
docs.text: Start of the interval

docs.title: Slot: Lag Alignment Applied (lag_alignment_applied)
docs.text:
Whether and how values were lag-aligned to a clinical event. Relocated from envar_temporal: lag alignment attaches a value to an event (a linkage concern), not an intrinsic temporal property. See lag_alignment_specifier for the concrete lag value(s).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: If values were already shifted relative to the clinical event and this is not recorded, an analyst may apply the lag again — double-lagging is a silent analytic error that misdates every exposure.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Health effects can trail exposure by days — a heat wave today may send someone to hospital next week — so analyses sometimes pair a clinical event with earlier exposure values. This records whether such a shift was already built into the data.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Lag Alignment Is Missing (lag_alignment_applied_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason lag_alignment_applied is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Records why the lag-alignment status is unknown; without it analysts cannot tell whether the data is safely unlagged or the documentation simply has not caught up, leaving the double-lagging risk open.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the lag field is empty, this says why — for example because the documentation is still being written.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Lag Alignment Is Missing (lag_alignment_applied_missing_reason)

Slot: Lag Alignment Specifier (lag_alignment_specifier)
docs.text:
Free-form specifier paired with lag_alignment_applied to capture the concrete lag values (e.g. "3" for a 3-day lag, or "0-21" for a distributed lag from 0 to 21 days). Empty when lag_alignment_applied = none.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Knowing that a lag was applied is useless without the concrete value(s); a 3-day lag and a distributed lag over 0-21 days define entirely different exposure windows and cannot be reconstructed after the fact.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: This spells out exactly how many days the exposure values were shifted relative to the clinical event — for example 3 days, or a whole range like 0 to 21 days.

docs.title: Slot: Buffer Aggregation Method (linkage_buffer_aggregation_method)
docs.text:
Aggregation method applied within the buffer (mean / max / median / area-weighted mean).

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Within the same buffer, mean, max, and area-weighted mean yield different exposure values; omitting the method makes the assigned value irreproducible and cross-study comparisons unsafe.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If several map values fall inside the circle around a home, they must be boiled down to one number. This records how — for example by taking the average, or the highest value.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Buffer Aggregation Is Missing (linkage_buffer_aggregation_method_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason linkage_buffer_aggregation_method is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Separates "not applicable — no buffer aggregation was performed" from an undocumented gap; without the reason a null method leaves the linkage unauditable.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the how-values-were-combined field is empty, this says why.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Buffer Aggregation Is Missing (linkage_buffer_aggregation_method_missing_reason)

Slot: Buffer Radius (Metres) (linkage_buffer_radius_m)
docs.text:
Buffer radius in metres for buffer-aggregation strategies.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: For buffer strategies the radius defines the exposure footprint: a 500 m and a 5 km buffer around the same residence can average over very different air or heat conditions, so the assigned value is not reproducible without it.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When exposure is averaged over a circle drawn around a person's home, this is how wide that circle is, in metres.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (linkage_buffer_radius_m_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason linkage_buffer_radius_m is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "no buffer applies because the strategy is point extraction" from "the radius was simply not recorded" — without it a null radius is ambiguous and the linkage cannot be audited.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the circle-width field is empty, this says why — for example because no circle was used at all.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (linkage_buffer_radius_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (linkage_buffer_radius_m_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Buffer Radius Is Missing (linkage_buffer_radius_m_missing_reason)

Slot: Maximum Distance to Station (Metres) (linkage_max_distance_to_station_m)
docs.text:
Maximum distance to a station for nearest-station strategies; values beyond this distance get null.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: For nearest-station strategies this cutoff decides whether a distant monitor still counts as "nearby"; beyond it the assignment is meaningless and should be null. Without the cutoff, values assigned from stations tens of kilometres away are indistinguishable from tight matches, silently degrading exposure quality.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Some methods take the reading from the closest measuring station. This is the farthest a station may be from the home before the match is considered too unreliable and no value is assigned.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Station Distance Is Missing (linkage_max_distance_to_station_m_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason linkage_max_distance_to_station_m is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "not applicable — the data is gridded, not station-based" from an undocumented cutoff; a bare null hides whether unlimited-distance station matches were allowed.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the maximum-station-distance field is empty, this says why.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Station Distance Is Missing (linkage_max_distance_to_station_m_missing_reason)
