Slot: Linkage Method (linkage_method)
docs.text:
Linkage-method object — see envar_linkage.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Attaching a place-based value to a person is a lossy, choice-laden step (geocoding, point vs buffer extraction, date alignment) — the "linkage descriptor" gap the GECC/EIRENE forum names as central. Without it, two studies using the same data product can differ solely through undocumented joins.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Environmental data describes places, but health research is about people. This block records how the value for a place was attached to a particular person — for example, by looking up the map cell containing their home address — since that step involves real choices that affect the result.

docs.title: Slot: Linkage Strategy (linkage_strategy)
docs.text:
How a gridded value is attached to a patient location.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The strategy determines which grid cells or stations contribute to a patient's value: point extraction, buffer aggregation, and population weighting can assign materially different exposures to the same address. Without it the person-level value cannot be reproduced or compared across studies — this is the "linkage descriptor" gap named by the GECC/EIRENE forum.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Environmental data comes as a map of values, but health data belongs to people. This slot records the rule used to pick a person's value off that map — for example reading the value exactly at their home, or averaging the values in a circle around it.

docs.title: Slot: Linkage Working CRS (linkage_working_crs)
docs.text:
Coordinate reference system the point-to-cell join was actually executed in, as an EPSG identifier or PROJ string, when it differs from the native SpatialReference.crs — e.g. reprojecting WGS84 points into an equal-area CRS before the spatial join.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Reprojection can move a point across a cell boundary: two runs with the same native CRS but different working CRS are different linkages that can assign different exposures to the same address. Recording the working CRS makes the join reproducible rather than deployment-dependent.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Before matching patient locations to map tiles, software sometimes converts all coordinates into a different mapping system that is better for measuring areas. This names that intermediate system, because the choice can nudge a location into a neighbouring tile.

docs.title: Slot: location
docs.text: Spatial metadata specifying where the observation was made - geolocation, place name, site id, environment, or biome

docs.title: Slot: methodology
docs.text: Information about how an observation was made - method, instrument, reagent kit, statistical modifier

docs.title: Slot: Metric Temporal Aggregation Rule (metric_temporal_aggregation_rule)
docs.text:
For heat-wave flags, how multi-day exposures are stamped on individual days (e.g. first_day_of_event / each_day_of_event / last_day_of_event).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Stamping a five-day event only on its first day versus on every day changes which person-days count as exposed, directly altering effect estimates in daily time-series and case-crossover designs.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A heat wave spans several days but health records are kept day by day; this says which calendar days actually get the "heat wave" label — just the first day, every day, or the last.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Aggregation Rule Is Missing (metric_temporal_aggregation_rule_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason metric_temporal_aggregation_rule is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "no multi-day stamping rule applies to this metric" from "a rule was applied but never captured" — only the latter makes the daily exposure series ambiguous.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Aggregation Rule Is Missing (metric_temporal_aggregation_rule_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregation Rule Is Missing (metric_temporal_aggregation_rule_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregation Rule Is Missing (metric_temporal_aggregation_rule_missing_reason)

Slot: Reason Aggregation Rule Is Missing (metric_temporal_aggregation_rule_missing_reason)
Slot: Missing Data Handling Method (missing_data_handling_method)
docs.text:
How the source handles missing values (e.g. how Daymet handles snow-covered pixels).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: How gaps were filled is often invisible downstream: an interpolated value looks identical to a measured one, so without this field an analyst overstates coverage and treats imputed exposures as if they were observed, biasing associations in unknown directions. It is the difference between apparent and real completeness.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Real data has holes — a cloud blocks a satellite, snow covers a sensor. This says what the dataset did about those holes: leave them empty, guess from nearby days and places, copy the last known value, and so on. Filled-in numbers can look just like real measurements, so it matters to know which is which.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Handling Method Is Missing (missing_data_handling_method_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason missing_data_handling_method is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Separates "the producer never documented how gaps were handled" from an entry someone forgot. Without the reason, a blank handling method is ambiguous and an analyst cannot judge whether the gap-filling behaviour is unknown or simply unrecorded here.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the gap-handling method is left empty, this explains why — often because the original dataset never said how it dealt with missing values. It makes the unknown explicit instead of a silent blank.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Handling Method Is Missing (missing_data_handling_method_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Handling Method Is Missing (missing_data_handling_method_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Handling Method Is Missing (missing_data_handling_method_missing_reason)

Slot: Reason Value Is Missing (missing_reason)
docs.text:
Controlled reason a nullable slot is null. Every nullable slot in the EnVar schemas should be paired with a *_missing_reason slot taking this range. CF-Conventions "missing_value with reason" pattern lifted to the metadata layer.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: An unexplained empty field is ambiguous: it could be a terminal gap in the source product, a fixable extraction gap, or a pipeline bug that dropped data. Recording the reason lets completeness checkers and roadmap planning tell these apart instead of treating every blank as equally hopeless.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When a box on a form is left empty, it helps to know why — was the question not applicable, did nobody ask it, or did the answer get lost on the way? This slot records that "why" instead of leaving a silent blank.

docs.title: Slot: Missing Value Sentinel (missing_value_sentinel)
docs.text:
The stored value that means "missing" in the source product's files, verbatim, e.g. 32767 (gridMET _FillValue) or -9999 (Daymet nodata). Complements missing_data_handling_method: the policy slot says how gaps were handled, this says which number encodes a gap.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A sentinel read as data is a catastrophic outlier (−9999 °C) or a silent bias (32767 packed); anyone touching the source files rather than the cleaned companion file needs to know which value to mask.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Datasets mark holes with an agreed impossible number, like −9999. If software doesn't know the marker, it will average it in as if it were a real reading. This field records the marker.

docs.title: Slot: Model Aggregate Uncertainty (model_aggregate_uncertainty)
docs.text:
Summary statistics for the model as a whole — cross-validation metrics and where they are reported.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Per-value uncertainty is often absent, so the whole-model cross-validation summary (R², RMSE) is frequently the only quantitative handle on how accurate the product is. Without it an analyst cannot judge whether the exposure estimates are precise enough for the health question, and cannot compare the reliability of two products.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A report card for how well the model predicts reality overall, checked by holding some data back and seeing how close its guesses came — for example an R² near 1 means the model tracks the true values closely. It also records where those scores were published.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason model_aggregate_uncertainty is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Separates "the producer published no cross-validation metrics" from a forgotten entry. Without the reason, a missing model-accuracy summary looks like a data-entry lapse, and reviewers cannot tell whether the information was ever available.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the model-accuracy summary is missing, this says why — usually because the people who made the dataset never reported those scores. It makes the absence deliberate and explainable rather than a mystery blank.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Aggregate Uncertainty Is Missing (model_aggregate_uncertainty_missing_reason)
Slot: Native Resolution Descriptor (native_spatial_resolution_descriptor)
docs.text:
Human-readable label for the native resolution, e.g. "1 km regular grid", "H3 hex zoom 8", "census tract polygon", "point station".

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The numeric resolution alone cannot distinguish a regular grid from hex cells, polygons, or point stations, and these layouts imply different extraction and error behaviour. Omitting the label leaves the geometry of the source data ambiguous even when the cell size is known.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A short plain-English phrase saying what the map data actually looks like — evenly spaced square tiles, honeycomb-shaped cells, irregular neighbourhood outlines, or individual measuring stations dotted around.

docs.title: Slot: Native Spatial Resolution (m) (native_spatial_resolution_m)
docs.text:
Native spatial resolution of the source product in metres. Daymet = 1000, GridMET ≈ 4000, NARR ≈ 32000.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Exposure misclassification scales directly with cell size: a value from a 32 km NARR cell averages over an entire metro area while a 1 km Daymet cell resolves a neighbourhood. Without the resolution, downstream users cannot judge how precise a per-patient exposure really is or whether two studies' values are comparable.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Gridded environmental data divides the world into square tiles, like pixels in a photo, and reports one value per tile. This field says how wide each tile is in metres — small tiles give a sharp picture of local conditions, big tiles give a blurry average over a large area.

docs.title: Slot: Native Units (UCUM) (native_units_ucum)
docs.text:
UCUM unit of the values as the source product stores them, when that differs from units_ucum (the unit of the values in the companion data file). Omit when the source already stores values in units_ucum — absence means "no conversion happened". Declaring it makes unit_conversion_formula required.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A value of 308 and a value of 35 can be the same temperature. When the source stores different units (or packed integers) than the companion file carries, the native unit is the anchor that lets a consumer check the conversion and re-read the source bytes; without it the sidecar's values cannot be traced back to the product.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Data producers often store numbers in different units than the ones shown to users — Kelvin instead of Celsius, or compressed integers that need unpacking. This field records the unit the source actually uses, so anyone going back to the original files knows what the raw numbers mean.

docs.title: Slot: Native Value Column (native_value_column)
docs.text:
Name of a second column carrying the same values in the source's native units (VariableIdentity.native_units_ucum), when the companion file ships both, e.g. Amadeus's value_kelvin next to value_celsius. value_column stays the binding for the values in units_ucum; this binds the native-unit twin.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: When a producer ships both unit representations, binding only one leaves an unexplained column in the data file and hides the cheapest available cross-check (recomputing one column from the other via unit_conversion_formula).

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Some data files helpfully include the same measurement twice, in two units — like a column in Kelvin next to one in Celsius. This names that second column so it is accounted for rather than mysterious.

docs.title: Slot: Native Value Offset (native_value_offset)
docs.text:
CF-style packing offset applied when decoding the source's stored integers to physical values (physical = stored × scale_factor + add_offset). Only meaningful alongside native_units_ucum.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The offset half of the unpacking convention; without it the scale factor alone still decodes to the wrong physical value.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: The second constant in the space-saving trick: after multiplying the stored number, add this to get the real measurement.

docs.title: Slot: Native Value Scale Factor (native_value_scale_factor)
docs.text:
CF-style packing multiplier applied when decoding the source's stored integers to physical values (physical = stored × scale_factor + add_offset). Only meaningful alongside native_units_ucum.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Anyone re-reading the source grid directly (rather than the companion file) must unpack stored integers with exactly this factor; a wrong or missing factor yields physically plausible but wrong values, the worst kind of error.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Big datasets often save space by storing small whole numbers that must be multiplied by a constant to get the real measurement. This is that constant.

docs.title: Slot: observation_result
docs.text: The outcome of the observation type - answer, value, result, determination

docs.title: Slot: observation_type
docs.text: Question being asked, measurement being taken, quality being observed

docs.title: Slot: offset
docs.text: Offset from event (e.g., "+3 days")

docs.title: Slot: Output File SHA-256 Hash (output_file_sha256)
docs.text:
SHA256 of the output file (CSV / parquet).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The output hash proves the companion data file is the one this metadata describes; without it, a swapped or regenerated file can silently detach the values from their provenance.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A fingerprint of the result file — change one byte and the fingerprint changes. It ties this metadata record to exactly one version of the data it describes.

docs.title: Slot: Output Row Count (output_row_count)
docs.text:
Number of rows in the output.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The expected output cardinality (e.g. 3 subjects × 153 days = 459 rows) is checkable at a glance; a mismatch flags dropped subjects or dates before any analysis runs on the data.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: The number of lines of data produced. Outputs often have a predictable size, so a wrong count is an early warning that rows went missing along the way.

docs.title: Slot: Partial-Day Attribution Rule (partial_day_attribution_rule)
docs.text:
How boundary / transition days of the patient's trajectory (trip start / end, travel days) are attributed when location changes within a day. The temporal partner to the known_travel_interval address alignment.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: On a transition day the patient is in two places; whether that day is credited to the origin, the destination, both, or excluded changes which exposure value the day receives, and an undocumented rule makes the linkage irreproducible.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If someone leaves on a trip mid-day, which place's environment "counts" for that day? This records the choice — the place they left, the place they arrived, both, or neither.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Attribution Rule Is Missing (partial_day_attribution_rule_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason partial_day_attribution_rule is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Separates "not applicable — the patient never changed location" from an undocumented handling of travel days; without it a null rule cannot be audited.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the travel-day rule field is empty, this says why.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Attribution Rule Is Missing (partial_day_attribution_rule_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Attribution Rule Is Missing (partial_day_attribution_rule_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Attribution Rule Is Missing (partial_day_attribution_rule_missing_reason)

Slot: Per-Value Uncertainty Type (per_value_uncertainty_type)
docs.text:
Kind of per-value uncertainty captured in the column named by DataLayout.value_uncertainty_column (see envar_layout).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A "±" number means nothing until you know what kind of number it is: a standard error, a 95 % prediction interval, and an ensemble spread are not interchangeable and cannot be pooled or propagated the same way. Without the type, downstream code either mishandles the uncertainty or drops it, so exposure measurement error goes unaccounted for and health-effect estimates are biased, usually toward the null.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Every estimated value comes with a "how sure are we" number, but there are several different kinds. This says which kind you are looking at — for example a standard error (a ± number saying how far off the estimate could plausibly be) versus a prediction interval (a range the true value should fall inside, say 95 times out of 100).

docs.title: Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason per_value_uncertainty_type is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Records the difference between "we know the source has no per-value uncertainty" and "someone forgot to fill this in". Without the reason, a blank uncertainty type is ambiguous and a validator cannot tell an honest gap from an oversight, so quality-completeness reporting is unreliable.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If the uncertainty-type field is left empty, this simply says why — for instance the original dataset never published one. It turns a confusing blank into an explicit, honest "not available, and here is the reason".

docs.title: Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Type Is Missing (per_value_uncertainty_type_missing_reason)
Slot: Uncertainty Units (UCUM) (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum)
docs.text:
Units of the per-value uncertainty in UCUM syntax. Usually the same as the value units.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: An uncertainty number is meaningless without its units: a per-value error of "2" is 2 °C or 2 K depending on this field, and a mismatch between value units and uncertainty units silently corrupts any error propagation into the health analysis.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: This says what the uncertainty is measured in — degrees Celsius, micrograms per cubic metre, and so on — written in a standard code (UCUM) that computers read the same way every time. Usually it matches the units of the value itself.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Uncertainty Units Are Missing (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "there is no uncertainty column, so units genuinely do not apply" from an accidental omission. Without the reason a blank units field is ambiguous and completeness checks cannot tell a legitimate not-applicable from a missing entry.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If the uncertainty-units field is blank, this explains why — often because there is no uncertainty column at all, so there is nothing to put units on. It stops a reader from wondering whether the units were simply forgotten.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Uncertainty Units Are Missing (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Units Are Missing (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Units Are Missing (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Units Are Missing (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Units Are Missing (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Units Are Missing (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Units Are Missing (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Uncertainty Units Are Missing (per_value_uncertainty_units_ucum_missing_reason)

Slot: Percentile Reference Geographic Scope (percentile_reference_geographic_scope)
docs.text:
Geographic scope over which the reference distribution was computed. One of local_tract, local_county, local_climate_region, national.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A 95th percentile computed over one county and one computed over the whole nation are very different thresholds; the scope is what encodes "hot for here", and without it the threshold cannot be reproduced or compared across studies.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: "Unusually hot" can mean hot compared to what is normal HERE, or compared to the whole country. This field says which area's history the comparison was made against.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason percentile_reference_geographic_scope is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "the source never stated its baseline geography" from "it was not extracted" — telling consumers whether the ambiguity is recoverable from the source or permanent.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Geographic Scope Is Missing (percentile_reference_geographic_scope_missing_reason)
Slot: Percentile Reference Period End (percentile_reference_period_end)
docs.text:
End of the reference distribution.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Together with the start date this pins down the reference distribution; an end date that includes or excludes the most recent warm years shifts the percentile threshold, so an open-ended baseline is not reproducible.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: The other end of the stretch of past years used as the comparison — start and end together say exactly which years define "normal".

docs.title: Slot: Reason Period End Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_end_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason percentile_reference_period_end is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "no percentile baseline applies to this metric" from "the baseline end was used but never recorded" — deciding whether the threshold is merely undocumented or genuinely absent.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Period End Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_end_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Period End Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_end_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Period End Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_end_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Period End Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_end_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Period End Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_end_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Period End Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_end_missing_reason)

Slot: Reason Period End Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_end_missing_reason)

Slot: Percentile Reference Period Start (percentile_reference_period_start)
docs.text:
Start of the reference distribution used for percentile-based thresholds. Mandatory for percentile metrics: "95th percentile" over 2000-2019 gives a different threshold than 1980-2010, and this delta is real.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: In a warming climate a "95th percentile" computed over 1971-2000 is a materially different threshold than one over 2000-2019; leaving the baseline unstated makes percentile-based studies non-comparable and non-reproducible.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: "Unusually hot" means hot compared to some stretch of past years. Which years you compare against changes what counts as unusual — this field records where that stretch begins.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Period Start Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_start_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason percentile_reference_period_start is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "not a percentile-based metric, so no baseline exists" from "a baseline was used but not captured" — without the reason, a consumer cannot tell a benign blank from a blocking one.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Period Start Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_start_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Period Start Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_start_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Period Start Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_start_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Period Start Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_start_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Period Start Is Missing (percentile_reference_period_start_missing_reason)

Slot: Percentile Reference Seasonal Window (percentile_reference_seasonal_window)
docs.text:
Seasonal window over which the reference was computed. One of annual, warm_season_may_sep, calendar_month.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: An annual reference distribution includes winter days and drags the 95th percentile far below a warm-season-only percentile; two thresholds that sound identical diverge substantially when the seasonal window is left unstated.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Whether "normal" includes the whole year or only the summer months changes what counts as unusually hot — a day that is extreme against summer-only history may be unremarkable against the full year.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Seasonal Window Is Missing (percentile_reference_seasonal_window_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason percentile_reference_seasonal_window is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "the source never stated its seasonal window" from "it was not extracted" — so consumers know whether asking the producer could still recover it.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Seasonal Window Is Missing (percentile_reference_seasonal_window_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Seasonal Window Is Missing (percentile_reference_seasonal_window_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Seasonal Window Is Missing (percentile_reference_seasonal_window_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Seasonal Window Is Missing (percentile_reference_seasonal_window_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Seasonal Window Is Missing (percentile_reference_seasonal_window_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Seasonal Window Is Missing (percentile_reference_seasonal_window_missing_reason)

Slot: PHI Status (phi_status)
docs.text:
Whether the sidecar carries any Protected Health Information. A record-level safety assertion; by design, sidecars are PHI-free.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Sidecars are designed to be PHI-free, and this slot is the explicit machine-readable assertion of that. Without it, every sharing, deposit, or export step must treat the document as potentially containing patient data and re-review it manually before it can leave a protected environment.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: PHI (protected health information) means private medical details about an identifiable person. This flag is a written promise on the document saying "no private patient information inside", so it can be passed around and published safely.

docs.title: Slot: Population Weighting Source (population_weighting_source)
docs.text:
Census vintage used for population weighting (e.g. the Spangler et al. WBGT product is population-weighted from gridMET). Null when no weighting is applied.

Tier: conditionally_core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Population-weighted values depend on which population dataset and census vintage supplied the weights; the same grid weighted by 2010 versus 2020 populations gives different exposures. Without the source, a weighted value cannot be reproduced or compared with other weighted products.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Some datasets average their tiles by giving more weight to places where more people live, so the number reflects what a typical resident experiences rather than a plain average over land. This field names the population map (and its year) that was used for that weighting.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Population Weighting Is Missing (population_weighting_source_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason population_weighting_source is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A blank weighting source is ambiguous between "no weighting was applied" and "the source was not recorded". Recording the reason keeps unweighted products from looking like incompletely documented weighted ones and lets automated checks pass legitimately null records.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When the population-weighting field above is empty, this field explains why — usually because the dataset is a plain average with no population weighting at all. It makes the emptiness intentional rather than an oversight.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Population Weighting Is Missing (population_weighting_source_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Population Weighting Is Missing (population_weighting_source_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Population Weighting Is Missing (population_weighting_source_missing_reason)

Slot: profile_version
docs.text: Version of microschema profile this conforms to

docs.title: Slot: Provenance Chain (provenance_chain)
docs.text:
Ordered W3C-PROV-style chain of upstream tool runs — see envar_toolrun. Recommended (not required): a record is reproducible in principle without the full chain, but real reproduction needs it.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The final tool run is rarely the whole story — downloads, geocoding, and intermediate transforms precede it; without the ordered chain back to the raw source, end-to-end reproduction and error tracing are impossible even when the last step is pinned.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Data usually passes through several tools before the final value appears — download, geocode, extract. This block lists those earlier steps in order, like a chain of custody reaching back to the original raw source.

docs.title: Slot: Provenance Chain Steps (provenance_chain_steps)
docs.text:
Ordered list of upstream ToolRuns, from oldest to most recent, whose outputs were inputs to this run.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A value like a daily temperature is usually the end of several runs (geocode, then extract); without the ordered steps a consumer can verify only the last hop, and any upstream error is invisible. The chain lets anyone walk the full derivation back to its root.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A step-by-step history of every program run that led to this data, oldest first — like a parcel's tracking history showing each station it passed through on the way to you.

docs.title: Slot: Provenance Chain Terminus Type (provenance_chain_terminus_type)
docs.text:
The kind of root of the chain.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A chain that just stops is unverifiable — stating whether it ends at a raw source download, synthetic data, or a pre-existing curated dataset tells consumers what kind of trust the root deserves, and makes the no-orphaned-steps validation rule checkable.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: This says what the very first link of the history is — freshly downloaded raw data, simulated data, or an existing curated dataset. Different starting points deserve different levels of scrutiny.

docs.title: Slot: Record Identifier (provenance_id)
docs.text:
Stable identifier for this sidecar / record (ULID recommended). This is the value the downstream health-data layer's source-value field carries to link a row back to its provenance (for OMOP, that field is external_exposure.exposure_source_value).

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: This identifier is what the downstream health-data layer's source-value field carries (for OMOP, external_exposure.exposure_source_value); it is the only hook that links an exposure value in the health layer back to its full spatial, temporal, and model provenance. Omit it and the value becomes untraceable — its metadata can never be recovered.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A unique serial number for this metadata document, like the tracking number on a parcel. Wherever the data value ends up, that number lets you look up exactly where it came from and how it was made.

docs.title: Slot: Quality Flag Column (quality_flag_column)
docs.text:
Name of any per-value QA flag column. CF ancillary_variables analogue; the flag vocabulary lives in the Uncertainty microschema.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Without knowing where the per-value QA flags live, consumers cannot filter or down-weight values the producer already marked as suspect, so known-bad measurements flow silently into analyses.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A quality flag column is like a traffic light next to each number — good, suspect, or bad — recorded by the data producer. This slot names that column; what the flag codes mean is described in the Uncertainty microschema.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason quality_flag_column is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Without a stated reason, a missing QA-flag column is ambiguous: the source may publish no flags, or the pipeline may have dropped them. The reason turns a silent gap into a documented decision that a reviewer can check against the upstream product.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: When there is no quality-flag column, this slot says why it is absent — for example because the source dataset simply does not publish quality flags.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Column Is Missing (quality_flag_column_missing_reason)
Slot: Quality Flag Vocabulary (quality_flag_vocabulary)
docs.text:
Reference to the QA flag vocabulary (e.g. an EPA AQS qualifier-code list) used by the column named in DataLayout.quality_flag_column (see envar_layout).

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: QA flags (e.g. "estimated", "below detection limit", "instrument malfunction") are only interpretable against the code list that defines them; without naming the vocabulary, a flagged value cannot be correctly filtered or trusted, so suspect measurements may enter the analysis unnoticed.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Some datasets tag individual values with short quality codes — think of the little footnote letters next to numbers in a table. This points to the key that explains what each code means, so you know which readings to trust or set aside.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Quality Flag Vocabulary Is Missing (quality_flag_vocabulary_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason quality_flag_vocabulary is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "this product has no QA flags at all" (common for gridded data) from an omission. Without the reason, an empty vocabulary field is ambiguous and a completeness check cannot tell an inapplicable entry from a missing one.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: If there is no quality-code key listed, this says why — often because the dataset simply does not use quality codes. It turns a blank into a clear statement rather than leaving the reader guessing.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Quality Flag Vocabulary Is Missing (quality_flag_vocabulary_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Vocabulary Is Missing (quality_flag_vocabulary_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Vocabulary Is Missing (quality_flag_vocabulary_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Quality Flag Vocabulary Is Missing (quality_flag_vocabulary_missing_reason)

Slot: quantity_unit
docs.text: Unit (UCUM, UO, QUDT, etc.)

docs.title: Slot: quantity_value
docs.text: The numeric value

docs.title: Slot: Recommended Citation (recommended_citation)
docs.text:
One-line recommended citation derived from the slots above.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: A ready-made citation lowers the barrier to correct attribution: without it, reusers assemble citations by hand, producing inconsistent references that break citation tracking and deprive the producers of credit for the deposit.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: This is the one-line "how to credit us" text, like the suggested citation on the back of a report. Anyone reusing the dataset can copy it verbatim into their paper.

docs.title: Slot: Record Identity & Safety (record_bookkeeping)
docs.text: Sidecar bookkeeping — the schema version the record conforms to, its stable identifier, and the record-level PHI safety assertion.

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Isolates the sidecar's own metadata (schema version, stable identifier, PHI assertion) from the exposure content, so version pinning and safety review can be done without reading the scientific fields.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Housekeeping about the record itself rather than the measurement: which version of the schema it follows, its permanent identifier, and a statement that it contains no private patient information.

docs.title: Slot: relative_to_event
docs.text: Event this is relative to

docs.title: Slot: Reporting Reference (reported_in)
docs.text: DOI / citation where the aggregate uncertainty is reported.

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Uncertainty numbers copied into a sidecar are only as trustworthy as their source. Without the reference the metrics cannot be verified, attributed, or updated when the producer revises them.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Where these numbers come from — the paper or report (ideally a DOI) so a reader can check them at the source.

docs.title: Slot: Run Arguments (run_arguments)
docs.text:
The exact argument map passed at invocation, serialised as a JSON string.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The same tool run with different arguments produces different outputs; without the exact argument map, re-running the tool reproduces the tool, not the result. Recording it verbatim removes all guesswork about settings.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: These are the settings the program was started with — like the knob positions on a machine. Writing them down means someone else can set the knobs identically before pressing start.

docs.title: Slot: Run Duration (Seconds) (run_duration_seconds)
docs.text:
How long the run took, in seconds.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Duration is a cheap sanity signal — a step that normally takes an hour finishing in two seconds hints at silent failure or truncated input. Omitting it loses a diagnostic, not reproducibility.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: How long the program ran, in seconds. A wildly unusual run time is often the first clue that something went wrong even when the tool reported success.

docs.title: Slot: Run Environment (run_environment)
docs.text:
Host OS, Docker / podman version, R / Python version, key library versions, as a native key/value object.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Library and runtime versions can change numeric results even when the tool version is fixed; recording the environment explains otherwise-mysterious differences between re-runs. For non-containerised tools it is the only record of the software stack.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A snapshot of the computer setup around the tool — operating system, language versions, key libraries. Like noting the oven and altitude alongside a recipe, because the surroundings can change the outcome.

docs.title: Slot: Run Log Excerpt (run_log_excerpt)
docs.text:
Last ~50 lines of the run log, where useful for debugging or audit.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The log tail captures warnings and error summaries that no structured field carries; without it, diagnosing a suspect run means hunting for logs that may no longer exist. Omission costs auditability, not reproducibility.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: The last few lines the program printed while running — its own account of what it did and whether anything looked wrong. Keeping a snippet is like stapling the receipt to the record.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason run_log_excerpt is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Stating why the log excerpt is absent (e.g. logs exist on the runner but are not captured) distinguishes a deliberate omission from data loss and tells auditors where to look. A blank is a bug; a null-with-reason is information.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A note explaining why no log snippet is attached — often the logs live on the machine that ran the job and were simply never copied into the record.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Log Excerpt Is Missing (run_log_excerpt_missing_reason)
Slot: Run Timestamp (UTC) (run_timestamp_utc)
docs.text:
When the run started (UTC).

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: The timestamp orders runs within the provenance chain and lets auditors match the record against external logs and the state of upstream data sources at that moment. Without it, "which run produced this file" can become undecidable.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Simply when the program started, expressed on a single worldwide clock (UTC) so that times recorded on different machines in different time zones line up.

docs.title: Slot: Schema Version (schema_version)
docs.text:
The version of the EnVar micro-schema this document conforms to. Required on every sidecar so downstream consumers can branch on schema evolution.

Tier: core

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Downstream consumers must branch on schema evolution: a parser built for one version will silently misread or wrongly reject sidecars written against another. Without this slot there is no way to tell which iteration of the schema a document targets.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: Schemas change over time, like editions of a paper form. This is the edition number stamped on the document, so anyone reading it knows exactly which version of the form was filled in and can interpret the fields accordingly.

docs.title: Slot: Solar Radiation Basis (solar_radiation_basis)
docs.text:
For WBGT / UTCI inputs, the basis used for solar radiation.

Tier: recommended

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: WBGT and UTCI respond strongly to solar load; whether the sun input was a measured shortwave flux, a modelled mean radiant temperature, or absent (triggering a fallback formula) changes the value and whether two records are comparable at all.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: How much sunshine hits a person is part of how hot they feel, and datasets represent "sunshine" in different ways — or not at all. This says which representation fed the calculation.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Radiation Basis Is Missing (solar_radiation_basis_missing_reason)
docs.text:
Reason solar_radiation_basis is null.

Tier: optional

docs.title: Why this slot matters
docs.text: Distinguishes "the source never stated its radiation basis" from "it was not extracted" — deciding whether the ambiguity can be resolved by going back to the source or is permanent.

docs.title: In plain terms
docs.text: A blank field is ambiguous: it could mean the thing does not exist, or that nobody wrote it down. This companion field says which one it is.

docs.title: Slot: Reason Radiation Basis Is Missing (solar_radiation_basis_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Radiation Basis Is Missing (solar_radiation_basis_missing_reason)
Slot: Reason Radiation Basis Is Missing (solar_radiation_basis_missing_reason)
